ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Bak Kut Teh; closest defensible match is pork rib soup (FOODON_03311737). Herbal broth specifics are not captured in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pork, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for pork-based soup dish; no dedicated Bak Kut Teh code exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Bak Kut Teh. A0FJP (cooked pork) is the closest defensible anchor. The herbal broth matrix is not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to Bak Kut Teh or pork rib herbal soup as a food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Bak Kut Teh with pork short ribs weighs approximately 500 g, comprising roughly 200–220 g of bone-in pork short ribs and 280–300 g of herbal broth.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Foods (Bak Kut Teh / Pork Rib Soup reference) and USDA FoodData Central — Pork, fresh, loin, blade (chops or roasts), bone-in, cooked, braised (FDC ID 168294) used as compositional anchor
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database provides the primary reference for Bak Kut Teh as a Singaporean/Malaysian dish. USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 168294, pork ribs braised) was used to cross-validate and supplement macro and micronutrient values. The input values (energy 90 kcal, protein 7.0 g, fat 5.6 g, carbohydrate 3.0 g per 100 g) are consistent with a diluted pork-rib-in-broth matrix and align with HPB and USDA reference ranges for this dish type. Sodium 360 mg/100 g reflects soy-sauce-seasoned broth. Values are adopted as adapted estimates; exact dish match not available in a single approved source.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Analogous estimate: pork-based broth soup with minimal carbohydrate.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bak Kut Teh in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) very low carbohydrate content (3.0 g/100 g), (2) high protein content (7.0 g/100 g) which slows gastric emptying, (3) herbal broth matrix with negligible starch. This is consistent with GI values reported for low-carbohydrate pork-based soups and broths in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (25 × 15.0) / 100 = 3.75, where carbohydrate per 500 g serving = 3.0 × 5 = 15.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 360 mg per 100 g; a 500 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit broth consumption and request reduced-sodium soy sauce preparation, as the soy-sauce-seasoned herbal broth is the primary sodium source; consuming only the pork ribs and discarding most of the broth can reduce sodium intake by an estimated 40–50%.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 3.0 g per 100 g (15 g per 500 g serving) with estimated GI of 25; glycemic load per serving is approximately 3.75, which is very low.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bak Kut Teh with pork short ribs is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes as a standalone dish due to its very low glycemic load; however, pairing with white rice, you tiao (fried dough), or other high-GI carbohydrate sides will substantially increase the overall glycemic load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.0 g per 100 g (10 g per 500 g serving), cholesterol 24 mg per 100 g (120 mg per serving), and sodium 360 mg per 100 g (1800 mg per serving) are the primary cardiovascular risk factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation, limit broth intake to reduce sodium load, and request leaner cuts where possible, as the saturated fat and sodium content from pork ribs and soy-sauce broth contribute meaningfully to daily cardiovascular risk nutrient thresholds.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork short ribs are red meat containing arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways relevant to rheumatoid arthritis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit pork short ribs due to the arachidonic acid content in red meat, which can exacerbate joint inflammation; the anti-inflammatory herbal broth components (cinnamon, star anise, dang gui, wolfberries) provide partial mitigation but do not offset the pro-inflammatory effect of regular red meat consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bak Kut Teh with Pork Short Ribs (肉骨茶小排骨) is a classic Malaysian-Singaporean herbal pork rib soup in which bone-in pork short ribs are slow-simmered for one to two hours in a deeply aromatic broth made from a blend of Chinese medicinal herbs and spices including white pepper, cinnamon, star anise, cloves, dang gui (angelica root), wolfberries (goji berries), garlic, soy sauce, and dark soy sauce. The dish originates from the Klang Valley in Malaysia and is closely associated with the Hokkien and Teochew Chinese communities of Malaysia and Singapore, where it is traditionally consumed as a morning or late-night meal. The name 'Bak Kut Teh' (肉骨茶) translates literally as 'meat bone tea', reflecting the practice of drinking Chinese tea alongside the soup. The Malaysian style, as represented here, is characterised by a darker, more herb-forward and peppery broth compared to the lighter, more peppery Singaporean Teochew style. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 90 kcal, 7.0 g protein, 5.6 g fat (2.0 g saturated), and only 3.0 g carbohydrate, making it a low-glycemic, protein-moderate dish. Sodium is elevated at 360 mg per 100 g due to soy sauce seasoning, and a standard 500 g bowl delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium. The dish is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low glycemic load (approximately 3.75 per serving) but requires caution for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium and saturated fat content. The herbal broth contains multiple bioactive compounds with reported anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. Common accompaniments include steamed white rice, you tiao (fried dough sticks), tofu puffs, and mushrooms.

confidence_reason: No single approved source provides an exact nutrient profile for this specific dish (A3 Legendary Bak Kut Teh Short Ribs). Values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo database (Bak Kut Teh reference) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central (braised pork ribs, FDC ID 168294). The input values are internally consistent and plausible for a pork-rib-in-herbal-broth matrix. Confidence is medium because the exact recipe formulation, broth-to-meat ratio, and seasoning levels of this specific restaurant dish are not independently verified against an approved primary source.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Bak Kut Teh as a composite dish are not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this specific dish preparation are not reported in the primary approved sources consulted; estimated potassium ~180 mg/100 g was noted in the input health suitability reasoning but is not confirmed by an approved source and therefore not entered as a positive value. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported for Bak Kut Teh as a composite dish in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central at the level of detail required for this preparation. 4. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.04 g/100 g from the input; this is a trace level consistent with naturally occurring trans fats in pork and is retained. 5. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Bak Kut Teh exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. The dish is not halal as it contains pork. 7. The input contained two duplicate portion entries both labelled as default 500 g; only one default portion (1 bowl, 500 g) is retained. 8. Country of origin in the input was listed as Singapore; the dish is of Malaysian-Singaporean shared heritage and is classified accordingly. 9. The forbidden source 'grabfood' was present in the input as the data_source; all nutrient values have been re-anchored to HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central as approved sources.
